coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots-labeled epitope imprinted polymer with double templates via the metal chelation for specific recognition of cytochrome c
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Yun-Jing
He, Xi-Wen
Li, Wen-You
Zhang, Yu-Kui
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescent sensor nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)/SiO2/molecular imprinting polymer（N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP）was fabricated by surface imprinting and epitope imprinting to recognize and detect the target protein cytochrome c (Cyt C) with fluorescence quenching. In the polymerization process, the C- and N-terminal nonapeptides of Cyt C were selected as the double templates which were fixed by functional monomer (zinc acrylate) through metal chelation and steady six-membered ring. The linear range of fluorescence quenching for this receptor towards Cyt C was 0.20–60μM, and the detection limit was 0.11μM. The precision for six times replicate determination of Cyt C at 30μM was 1.20%, and the imprinting factor (IF) was 3.06. The recoveries of the material to Cyt C in urine were 99.3–114.0%. In brief, this work proposed a strategy to prepare a new type fluorescent imprinting polymer based on N-GQDs and provided an attractive perspective for the detection of protein by using the combination of N-GQDs and molecular imprinting technique.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Surface imprinting
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Epitope imprinting
Metal chelation
Fluorescent sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the N-GQDs /SiO2/MIP
Optosensing of Cyt C by the N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP
Effects of the amount of functional monomer zinc acrylate and cross-linker EGDMA
The response of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP to Cyt C

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin City

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21275078 and 21475069) and the Tianjin Natural Science Foundation (No. 16JCZDJC37200).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the synthesis protocol of the N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP.

HRTEM images of N-GQDs (a), N-GQDs/SiO2 (b) (inset: the high magnification HRTEM image of N-GQDs/SiO2), N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP (c), and the high magnification HRTEM image of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP (d). The energy-dispersive X-ray spectra (EDS) of N-GQDs/SiO2 (e) and N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP (f), the Cu peak in the spectrum comes from the copper grid. Insets were the surface scan of C, N, O, Si, Zn (A), and Zn (B).
The effects of the amount of functional monomer Zn(CH2=CHCOO)2 (a) and cross-linker EGDMA (b) on the fluorescence quenching and imprinting factor of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP to Cyt C.

The fluorescence emission spectra of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP (a), N-GQDs/SiO2/NIP (b), N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP-1 (c), N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP-2 (d), N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP-3 (e), N-GQDs/SiO2/NIP-1 (f) with the addition of the indicated concentrations of Cyt C (0–60μM). Inset graphs showed the Stern-Volmer plots for N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP (a), N-GQDs/SiO2/NIP (b), N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP-1 (c), N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP-2 (d), N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP-3 (e), N-GQDs/SiO2/NIP-1 (f). Experimental conditions: the concentrations of all the polymers are 0.5mg/mL, V=1.0mL, pH=7.3 (PBS, 10mM), Ex=350nm.

Response of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP and N-GQDs/SiO2/NIP to different proteins (a); The FL intensity changes of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP under different concentration of BSA (b) or Lyz (c) and the fixed concentration of target protein Cyt C.
Analytical results for the determination of Cyt C in 100-fold dilution urine.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots-labeled epitope imprinted polymer with double templates via the metal chelation for specific recognition of cytochrome c

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Chemistry, Research Center for Analytical Sciences, State Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemical Biology, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Biosensing and Molecular Recognition, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Research Center for Analytical Sciences, State Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemical Biology, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Biosensing and Molecular Recognition, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescent sensor nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)/SiO2/molecular imprinting polymer（N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP）was fabricated by surface imprinting and epitope imprinting to recognize and detect the target protein cytochrome c (Cyt C) with fluorescence quenching. In the polymerization process, the C- and N-terminal nonapeptides of Cyt C were selected as the double templates which were fixed by functional monomer (zinc acrylate) through metal chelation and steady six-membered ring. The linear range of fluorescence quenching for this receptor towards Cyt C was 0.20–60μM, and the detection limit was 0.11μM. The precision for six times replicate determination of Cyt C at 30μM was 1.20%, and the imprinting factor (IF) was 3.06. The recoveries of the material to Cyt C in urine were 99.3–114.0%. In brief, this work proposed a strategy to prepare a new type fluorescent imprinting polymer based on N-GQDs and provided an attractive perspective for the detection of protein by using the combination of N-GQDs and molecular imprinting technique.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecular imprinting technique (MIT) presented by Wulff et al. in 1970s (Wulff et al., 1973) is a promising method to specifically recognize target by tailor-made, artificial binding sites with memory of the shape, size and functional group of the template molecule (Chen et al., 2011). Successful applications of molecular imprinting polymer (MIP) have been realized in chem/biosensing (Abdel-Haleem et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2014), antibody mimetics (Hoshino et al., 2010; Ye and Mosbach, 2008), catalysis (Guo and Guo, 2013), solid phase extraction (Lenain et al., 2012), and drug delivery (Xu et al., 2013a; Zhang et al., 2015). In the following decades, although MIP for small molecule synthesis is straightforward now (Anderson et al., 2005; Gao et al., 2007; Ge et al., 2013; Ma et al., 2013; Xu et al., 2013c), for biomolecules like protein imprinting is still a significant challenge. There are several major problems of protein leading to this situation. First, they have high molecular weights, which restricts the removal of the template protein. Then, conformational flexibility and high surface complexity result in low stability in non-physiological conditions, such as high/low temperature/pH, organic solvent, which hinders the imprinting process. Furthermore, it is difficult and expensive to obtain high pure proteins from nature source, which also blocks the development of protein imprinting (Whitcombe et al., 2011).

To overcome these problems, researchers adopted different strategies to prepare macromolecule-imprinted polymers including surface imprinting (He et al., 2005; Li et al., 2013a, 2014; Nematollahzadeh et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2013, 2011), metal-chelating imprinting (Chen et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2011; Qin et al., 2016), and epitope imprinting (Niu et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2015). The metal-chelating imprinting is a process of imprinting a template via metal ion chelating with the template (Xu et al., 2013b). We developed a thermosensitive macroporous hydrogel imprinted polymer to capture lysozyme (Qin et al., 2009), and the protein which had the surface-exposed imidazole groups (Im) of histidine was immobilized by Cu2+ ion chelated with N-(4-vinyl)-benzyliminodiacetic acid (VBIDA). Li and co-workers chose zinc acrylate as functional monomer, and used the chelation between the phosphate groups and Zn2+ ion to imprint tyrosine phosphopeptides (Li et al., 2013b). Epitope imprinting was first proposed by Rachkov and Minoura and has attracted more and more attention (Nishino et al., 2006; Rachkov and Minoura, 2000, 2001). The strategy was that only an exposed domain (epitope) part of protein was used as the template to form the binding sites instead of the whole protein. This method obtains several advantages compared with traditional protein imprinting. First, epitope has small size which reduces the mass transfer resistance when it is removed from the imprinting site. Second, the choices of reaction conditions and solvents are abundant. Third, the amino acid sequence of epitope can be commercially synthesized, which decreases the cost. Following this method, Decha and co-workers developed an ultrathin molecularly imprinted polymer on the gold-based transducer surface by using C-terminal nonapeptide of Cyt C as the template to enrich the target protein (Dechtrirat et al., 2012). Recently, we also fabricated an epitope thermo-sensitive MIP on the surface of silicon-modified carbon quantum dots to detect Cyt C (Li et al., 2016).

In recent years, MIT combining quantum dots (QDs) (e.g., CdTe QDs (Chao et al., 2014), CdSe/ZnS QDs (Liu et al., 2015), ZnS: Mn2+ QDs (Zhao et al., 2012)) have received extensive interests for the high sensitivity of QDs and the high selectivity of MIT. However, the inherent defects of those heavy metal quantum dots, potential toxicity and environmental hazards hindered their wide application (Yan et al., 2010). So, exploring eco-friendly alternatives is imminent. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as recently emerging luminescent carbon materials, have received increasing attention and were applicable in bioimaging, photovoltaic devices, and sensors (Shen et al., 2012), due to their attractive properties, including excellent photostability, biocompatibility, small size, highly tunable photoluminescence (PL) property, electrochemiluminescence (Zheng et al., 2015), eco-friendliness, and good water-solubility. Moreover, the doping of heteroatoms (typically doped by nitrogen atoms) can efficaciously improve the GQDs’ quantum yield (Lin et al., 2015) and lead to changes in their optical properties and electronic characteristics (Chen et al., 2016; Jin et al., 2013; Ju et al., 2016; Kong et al., 2016). Ju et al. reported a strategy for producing the surfactant-free Au nanoparticles (Au NPs) on N-GQDs by mixing N-GQDs and AuCl4 − without any surfactant for electrochemical detection of H2O2 (Ju and Chen, 2015). Xu and Chen used ZIF-8C as a new source of graphitic sheets to obtain N-GQDs to the fluorescent analysis of Fe3+(Xu et al., 2015). However, to the best of our knowledge, the fluorescence epitope imprinting receptor based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) for effectively capturing of protein has not been reported to date. Herein, we proposed a novel strategy with N-GQDs as fluorescent source, C- and N-terminal nonapeptides of Cyt C as the double templates based on epitope approach to synthesize MIP, relying on the use of N-GQDs fluorescence quenching to specifically recognize the target protein. The fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) may explain the fluorescence quenching phenomenon (Sapsford et al., 2006; Zhou et al., 2014).

In this report, N-GQDs was prepared, and then modified with silane, which played the role of the carrier of the polymer for the further procedure. Then, the imprinting layer was generated with the copolymerization of zinc acrylate and ethyleneglycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) initiated by azodiisobutyronitrile (AIBN) in the presence of the double templates, C-terminal nonapeptide (AYLKKATNE) and N-terminal nonapeptide (GDVEKGKKI), which was anchored on the surface of silica-modified N-GQDs, and the specific tailor-made imprinting cavities would be exposed after removing the templates. Zinc acrylate was chosen as the functional monomer, which can form steady six-membered ring with templates through the metal chelation and hydrogen bonding interaction, and the solid and stable interaction between functional monomer and the double templates was subservient to get more effective imprinting sites and then improved the imprinting factor. The prepared N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP demonstrated specific recognition toward Cyt C, and the material gathered the high sensitivity of QDs and the high selectivity of MIT. Therefore, the proposed strategy confirmed a feasible method to prepare a novel fluorescence imprinting receptor based on N-GQDs, surface imprinting procedure and epitope imprinting.

Citric acid, Tris, (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), ammonium hydroxide (NH3·H2O), ethyleneglycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA), azodiisobutyronitrile (AIBN) were purchased from J&K Chemical Co. Zinc acrylate were purchased from Alfa Aesar. N, N-dimethlformamide (DMF), ethanol, methanol (MeOH), acetic acid (HAc) were purchased from Concord Technology Co. Ltd (Tianjin, China). Disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O), sodium dihydrogen phosphate hydroxide (NaH2PO4·2H2O) were got from GuangFu Fine (Tianjin, China). All the reagents were analytical grade and without further purification.

The two nonapeptides chain AYLKKATNE and GDVEKGKKI were synthesized by GL Biochem Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Cytochrome c (Cyt C, MW 12.4kDa, pI 10.2), bovine serum album (BSA, MW 67.0kDa, pI 4.9), human serum albumin (HSA, MW 66.0kDa, pI 4.6), ovalbumin (OB, MW 45.0kDa, pI 4.7), lysozyme (Lyz, MW 13.4kDa, pI 11.2)，transferrin （Trf, MW 77kDa, pI 7.5）were purchased from Solarbio (Beijing, China). Urine was provided by healthy volunteers.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were got on a Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope with the 200kV accelerating voltage (FEI, Holland). The energy-dispersive X-ray spectra (EDS) were got on a Jem-2100F transmission electron microscope with the 200kV accelerating voltage (Japan). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra in KBr were recorded by using a FTS6000 spectrophotometer (Bio-rad, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of N-GQDs was measured on a Rigaku D/max/2500v/pc (Japan) with a Cu Kα source. UV–Vis absorption spectra (200–800nm) measurements were performed on a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence (FL) spectra were recorded on an F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The removing of template was analyzed by the high performance liquid chromatograph (HPLC, LC-20AD, Shimadzu, Japan), which equipped with a vacuum degasser, ternary pump (G1311B), and the variable wavelength detector system (G1314F) connected to a reversed-phase column (Eclipse Plus C18, 5µm, 4.6mm×250mm, Agilent, USA). The mobile phase for gradient elution were solvent A (TFA/MeCN, 0.1%, v/v) and solvent B (TFA/water, 0.1%, v/v) with a flow rate of 1mL/min.

The N-GQDs was prepared by a hydrothermal method according to the previous reference (Lin et al., 2015). Briefly, citric acid (2.0g) and Tris (0.5g) were mixed in a 25mL round-bottom flask, and then the mixture was heated at 225℃ for 15min. After the reaction, the molten reactor color changed to orange, and was cooled to room temperature. The obtained solid was dissolved in 5mL of ultrapure water, and then 1M NaOH solution was added drop by drop to neutralize the solution to pH about 7.5. Finally, the product was subjected to dialysis (retained molecular weight: 1000Da) overnight.

The silica-coated N-GQDs were synthesized by modified stöber method (Stöber et al., 1968). Briefly, 8mL of N-GQDs was dispersed into 25mL of ethanol solution, in which 60μL of APTES and 60μL of TEOS were added under magnetic stirring and the mixture was kept stirring for 3h. The obtained N-GQDs/SiO2 was centrifuged and then washed with ethanol for three times and then lyophilized.

50mg of N-GQDs/SiO2 were dissolved in 15mL of DMF, then 60mg of zinc acrylate and 10mg of C-terminal nonapeptide (AYLKKATNE) and 10mg of N-terminal nonapeptide (GDVEKGKKI) were added, which was preassembled for 1h under magnetic stirring at 65℃. Then 100μL of EGDMA was injected and the solution was deaerated by N2 for 30min. Next, 30mg of AIBN dispersed in 5mL of DMF was added to initiate the polymerization procedure, and the reaction was continued at 65℃ under magnetic stirring for 4h. The obtained polymers were centrifuged and then eluted with MeOH/HAc (9:1, v/v) until no template epitope was detected by HPLC. Finally, the resultant polymers were washed with ultrapure water, and were lyophilized. To compare the recognition capacity of the products, N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP −1, N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP −2 and N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP −3 were also synthesized by the following strategies: N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP-1, only C-terminal nonapeptide as the template; N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP-2, only N-terminal nonapeptide as the template; and N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP-3, acrylic acid as the functional monomer instead of zinc acrylate. Other procedures were same with those of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP.

The non -imprinting polymers (N-GQDs/SiO2/NIP) was prepared by the same method with N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP in the absence of the templates. The N-GQDs/SiO2/NIP-1 was prepared by the same method with N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP-3 in the absence of the templates.

In this experiment, all the fluorescence (FL) measurements were carried under the same conditions: the spectrofluorometer was set at fluorescence mode, the excitation wavelength was set at 350nm, both slit widths of the excitation and emission were 5nm, and the photomultiplier tube voltage was set at 700V. After adding appropriate N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP or N-GQDs/SiO2/NIP into the 2mL centrifuge tube, the given concentration of target protein Cyt C was added successively, and the mixture was diluted to the 1.0mL with phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH=7.3, 10mM). Then the mixture was mixed thoroughly and measured by the spectrofluorometer.

We used urine to investigate the potential application value in bioanalysis via standard addition recovery method. Urine was diluted to 100-fold with phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH=7.3, 10mM). After adding given concentration Cyt C, N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP was applied to detect the target protein in the real sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the N-GQDs /SiO2/MIP
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis process of the N-GQDs /SiO2/MIP is illustrated in Fig. 1. Firstly, N-GQDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method, and then were embedded into silica to form homogeneous silica shell, which has good optical transparency, water solubility, and biocompatibility. On the other hand, the silica layer could prevent the leak of N-GQDs and protect the fluorescence of N-GQDs. With C- and N-terminal nonapeptides of Cyt C as the double templates, the adsorption capacity of the target protein was increased. Moreover, the introducing of metal-chelating interaction made the binding of templates more solid and stable based on forming six-membered ring with zinc acrylate than those only by non-covalent interaction. Both of the above two points could enhance adsorption capacity of protein.

The HRTEM images are shown in Fig. 2. The size of N-GQDs, N-GQDs/SiO2, and N-GQDs /SiO2/MIP were about 3nm, 60nm and 78nm (Fig. 2a–d), respectively. The surface of N-GQDs /SiO2/MIP was obviously rough than that of N-GQDs /SiO2. That is to say, the imprinting layer was successfully anchored on the surface of N-GQDs /SiO2 and the thickness was about 18nm. In order to further display the fabrication process, the EDS spectroscopy analysis of N-GQDs/SiO2 and N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP were researched (Fig. 2e–f). Compared with the spectrum of N-GQDs/SiO2 (Fig. 2e), the signal of Zn2+(Kα) at 8.654keV appeared in the spectrum of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP (Fig. 2f), indicating the zinc acrylate as the functional monomer was modified on the surface of the N-GQDs/SiO2 successfully. The XRD pattern of N-GQDs is shown in Fig. S1 (Supporting Information. All of the following are the same). In the 2θ range from 10° to 60°, the characteristic peak of N-GQDs at around 22.0° matches with the graphitic structure (Tang et al., 2013), which indicated that the N-GQDs was synthesized successfully. FT-IR spectra of N-GQDs, N-GQDs/SiO2 and N-GQDs/ SiO2/ MIP are shown in Fig. S2. As shown in Fig. S2a (N-GQDs), the peaks at 3397, 2930, 1740cm−1 could be assigned to –OH/–NH stretching vibrations, C–H stretching vibrations, C˭C stretching vibrations, and the peak around 1452–1589cm−1 was ascribed to the aromatic rings skeletal vibrations, which confirmed the N-GQDs was successfully prepared. Compared with the spectrum of N-GQDs, the asymmetrical stretching vibration peak of Si-O-Si at 1046cm−1 and Si-O stretching vibration at 778cm−1 and 452cm−1 demonstrated that N-GQDs was cladded in silica layer (Fig. S2b). As shown in the spectrum of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP (Fig. S2c), the vibration of C˭O at 1732cm−1 was derived from zinc acrylate, which indicated that the imprinted polymer was synthesized successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optosensing of Cyt C by the N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP
ce:section.ce:para:
Cyt C had a quenching effect on the fluorescence of the N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP and N-GQDs/SiO2/NIP, and the fluorescence quenching can be described by the Stern–Volmer equation:
F
0
/ F = 1 + K
SV
[ Q ]
where F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP or N-GQDs /SiO2/NIP in the absence and presence of Cyt C, respectively, KSV was the fluorescence quenching constant, and [Q] was the concentration of the target protein. Imprinting factor (IF) was used to evaluate the imprinting effect which can be calculated by the formula: IF= KSV, MIP / KSV, NIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of the amount of functional monomer zinc acrylate and cross-linker EGDMA
ce:section.ce:para: The mass of functional monomer zinc acrylate and the cross-linker were optimized to achieve higher IF. In the optimization process, the concentration of the template was constant, and KSV was used as the index of fluorescence quenching value. At first, the amount of functional monomer was investigated and 60mg zinc acrylate was selected as the optimum dosage, as shown in Fig. 3a. When the mass of zinc acrylate was too small, it was not enough to combine more templates to form more imprinting cavities, thus less protein was adsorbed. However, when the amount of zinc acrylate was increased, the cross-linking degree of imprinted polymer would be reduced, and excess zinc acrylate were exposed to the surface of the particles, which made the nonspecific adsorption to be increased, as a result, the IF would be reduced. 100μL of EGDMA was chosen as the best amount, as shown in Fig. 3b. A lower degree of cross-linking would not be conducive to the formation of effective sites, while higher degree of cross-linking would hinder the removal of the template and the adsorption of the target protein.

The pH plays a significant role in rebinding target protein. Too acid or too alkaline will affect the activity and three-dimensional structure of the protein thus affecting the determination of protein. As shown in Fig. S3, since the specific recognition sites were formed in N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP, the change of pH has a greater influence on the fluorescence quenching amount of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP than that of N-GQDs /SiO2/NIP. The appropriate pH of rebinding was 7.3 (Fig. S3), and 10mM PBS buffer (pH=7.3) was used for further detection experiments.

Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP in the absence and presence of Cyt C were measured every 10min. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP in the absence and presence of Cyt C did not change significantly within 60min, which showed a stable emission of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP during this period. This result might indicate that the imprinted layer was anchored on the surface of N-GQDs /SiO2, and N-GQDs were protected by the imprinted layer effectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The response of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP to Cyt C
ce:section.ce:para: The further research of the binding properties of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP and N-GQDs/SiO2/NIP to Cyt C was revealed in Fig. 4. As shown in Figs. 4a and b, with increasing of the concentration of Cyt C, the FL intensity of the N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP and N-GQDs/SiO2/NIP decreased with a linear relationship, and at the same concentration of Cyt C, the decreased degree of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP was much larger than that of N-GQDs/SiO2/NIP. In general, the change of fluorescence intensity is related to the amount of adsorption of proteins, which is mainly determined by the specific binding force between the imprinted polymers and the target molecules. The tailor-made imprinting cavities were formed in N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP, so that more proteins can be adsorbed, and the fluorescence quenching for N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP was bigger than that for N-GQDs /SiO2/NIP. The linear range of fluorescence quenching for N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP towards Cyt C was 0.2–60μM, and the detection limit was 0.11μM. The precision of six times replicate determination of Cyt C at 30μM was 1.20%. Under the double templates condition, the imprinted factor (IF) of the N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP was 3.06. Compared with the imprinting effect of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP, the IF1 of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP-1 was 2.07 (Figs. 4c and b), the IF2 of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP-2 was 2.28 (Figs. 4d and b), and IF3 of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP-3 was 1.05 (Figs. 4e and f).

As can be seen in Fig. 4, C- and N-terminal nonapeptides of Cyt C as the double templates can reach a higher IF than that of only one terminal as the templates. The reasons are as follows: (1) Using the two-terminals nonapeptides as double templates could form two different kinds of spatial structure of the cavities; (2) The presence of two kinds of imprinted sites allowed the selective rebinding of either C- or N-terminal nonapeptide of the target protein into the imprinted sites of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP, respectively, while only one terminal of target protein was available into the imprinted cavities of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP-1 or N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP-2 when using C- or N-terminal nonapeptide as the template.

Compared with the N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP-3, the N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP has a higher IF, which should be attributed to using zinc acrylate as the functional monomer. There was only hydrogen bonding interaction between the template and the functional monomer when using acrylic acid as the functional monomer. However, when zinc acrylate was chosen as the functional monomer, it can interact with the templates through the metal chelation and six-membered ring, which were much stronger than hydrogen bonding interaction and thus can fix more templates and form more imprinted sites.

There is a great overlap between the UV–vis absorption band of Cyt C and the emission spectrum of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP (Fig. S5). When Cyt C rebinds to the imprinted sites, the energy would be transferred from N-GQDs(donor) to Cyt C (acceptor), which might be responsible for the mechanism of fluorescence quenching (Zhou et al., 2014).

In order to investigate the selectivity of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP, the fluorescent responses of this receptor toward diverse proteins were carried out. The response of N-GQDs /SiO2/MIP to Cyt C was strong, which results in a significant change in fluorescence intensity with higher KSV than other proteins ( Fig. 5a). The result showed that the N-GQDs /SiO2/MIP had a high specificity toward Cyt C. In the imprinting process, the tailor-made imprinted cavities with the memory of the size, shape, and specific chemical functions of the template molecules were generated, and the templates of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP were the “epitopes” of Cyt C, so the receptor showed a high ability to capture Cyt C. As we can see, for BSA, HSA, Lyz and OB, only a small KSV was caused, and the reason was that these proteins do not have the same C- or N-terminal nonapeptide with Cyt C, thus only a little FL intensity was quenched due to nonspecific adsorption. And the KSV of Trf was larger than that of other competitive proteins. The reason might be that Trf contained iron, which could cause the fluorescence quenching of quantum dots, and consequently the fluorescence quenching of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP by Trf would be stronger (Ju and Chen, 2014; Tam et al., 2014).

The competitive binding experiments were carried out by preparing binary protein solution of Cyt C-BSA and Cyt C-Lyz, respectively. The process was performed by fixing the concentration of Cyt C and changing the proportion of competitive protein to target protein. As shown in Figs. 5b and c, with the increase of the ratio of CBSA/CCyt C and CLyz/CCyt C (CBSA, CLyz and CCyt C are the concentrations of BSA, Lyz, and Cyt C, respectively), the FL intensity of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP had little change. Neither BSA nor Lyz has the same C- or N-terminal nonapeptide with Cyt C, so the fluorescence of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP could not be quenched by them. This experiment further testified the specific recognition ability of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP to Cyt C.

To explore the practicability of N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP in real sample, it was applied to detect Cyt C in urine. As shown in Table 1, the recoveries of these tests were 99.3–114.0%, which indicated that the fluorescent sensor synthesized by surface imprinting procedure and epitope imprinting was successfully applied in the determination of Cyt C in biological sample.

In this work, N-GQDs/SiO2/MIP as a novel fluorescent sensor was developed via surface imprinting procedure and epitope imprinting. C- and N-terminal nonapeptides of Cyt C were used as the double templates and metal chelation and six-membered ring between zinc acrylate and the double templates were utilized to immobilize templates, which played important roles in the formation of more imprinted sites and the improvement of imprinting factor. This receptor combined the virtue of the high sensitivity of QDs and the high selectivity of MIT, and was successfully applied in specific identification and detection of Cyt C in the biological sample. This method might have great application prospects in the determination of proteins by combing N-GQDs and MIT.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21275078 and 21475069) and the Tianjin Natural Science Foundation (No. 16JCZDJC37200).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2016.12.040.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
